ontology.foodex2_description: Fritters and similar deep-fried battered products
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated onion pakora code; A0CKP (fritters and similar deep-fried battered products) is the nearest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for onion fritters / pakora as a prepared food product.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of onion fritters consists of approximately 4 to 5 pieces weighing around 100 g, as commonly served as a starter or snack in Indian restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Onion Pakoda / Pakora (deep-fried besan-coated onion fritter)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) reports values for pakoda/pakora made from besan and vegetables including onion. Values for onion pakora are used as the primary reference. Minor adaptation applied to align with standard 100 g basis. Energy calculated from macros using Atwater factors where direct energy figure was cross-checked.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — chickpea flour-based fried snack / besan pakora analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for onion pakora exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 52 is estimated from the closest analogous food: chickpea flour (besan)-based fried snack, which has a reported GI of approximately 50–55 in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Deep-frying in oil slows gastric emptying and reduces glycemic response relative to baked or boiled chickpea products. GI 52 is used as a conservative mid-point estimate. GL per serving = (52 × 33.2 × 1.0) / 100 = 17.3 for a 100 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~320 mg per 100 g) and deep-fried preparation
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size of onion fritters due to moderate sodium content (~320 mg/100 g) and the additional sodium contributed by accompanying chutneys; occasional consumption of one small serving is acceptable within a low-sodium dietary pattern.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (GI 52) and significant carbohydrate load from chickpea flour batter
type2_diabetes.guidance: Onion fritters have a moderate-low GI of approximately 52 due to chickpea flour batter, but the glycemic load per 100 g serving is approximately 17, so individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict portion size to 50–60 g and pair with a low-GI accompaniment to blunt postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat content (~17.5 g/100 g) from deep-frying, with predominantly unsaturated fat profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Although the fat profile of onion fritters is predominantly unsaturated (monounsaturated and polyunsaturated from vegetable oil), the high total fat from deep-frying (~17.5 g/100 g) and moderate sodium content warrant limiting consumption to occasional small servings for individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Onion fritters, known in Hindi as Pyaaz ke Pakode or simply Pyaaz Pakora, are a popular deep-fried Indian snack made by coating thinly sliced onions in a spiced chickpea flour (besan) batter and frying until golden and crispy. The batter typically includes salt, red chilli powder, cumin, coriander, and sometimes ajwain (carom seeds), giving the fritters a savoury, mildly spicy flavour with a crunchy exterior and soft, sweet onion interior. They are a staple street food and restaurant starter across the Indian subcontinent and are widely served in Indian restaurants in Singapore, including at establishments such as Kinara at Boat Quay. Nutritionally, onion fritters are energy-dense (~310 kcal/100 g) due to deep-frying, with a moderate fat content (~17.5 g/100 g, predominantly unsaturated), significant carbohydrate from besan (~33 g/100 g), and moderate protein (~6.8 g/100 g). The chickpea flour base provides dietary fibre (~3.1 g/100 g), iron, folate, and B vitamins. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 52 (low-moderate), reflecting the slow-digesting nature of chickpea flour. Clinically, the sodium content (~320 mg/100 g) and caloric density from frying are the primary concerns for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. Onion fritters are vegetarian, vegan, and halal-suitable. They are typically served as a starter or snack with mint chutney or tamarind sauce.
common_user_queries: How many calories in onion bhaji?
What is the GI of onion fritters?
What flour is used in pakora?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) for besan-based pakora/pakoda, which is the closest approved primary source for this dish. GI is an analogous estimate from chickpea flour-based fried snack data in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. No exact entry for onion fritters exists in any approved primary source, hence medium confidence.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food. Cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as onion fritters are prepared with vegetable oil and contain no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN pakora data. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 as this is a fully plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients; naturally zero for vegan foods. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on chickpea flour-based fried snack data from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for onion pakora was found. Nutrient values represent a typical restaurant-style preparation; home recipes may vary. The food_group has been reclassified from 'Mixed' (input) to 'Snacks' to better reflect the nature of this deep-fried savoury snack.

improvement_actions_taken: Reclassified food_group from 'Mixed' to 'Snacks' to reflect the nature of the dish.
Added full multilingual local_names for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi as required for Indian cuisine in Singapore context.
Populated all per_100g nutrient fields using ICMR-NIN as primary source; -1 used for vitamin_d_mcg and selenium_mcg with documented justification in notes.
Set cholesterol_mg to 0.0 with confirmed zero justification (plant-based, vegetable oil only).
Set vitamin_b12_mcg to 0.0 with naturally zero justification (fully plant-based food).
GI set to 52 as analogous estimate from chickpea flour-based fried snack per University of Sydney GI Database; gi_category set to 'low' (52 ≤ 55).
GL per serving calculated as (52 × 33.2 × 1.0) / 100 = 17.3 for 100 g serving.
Ontology populated with closest_parent codes for FoodOn and FoodEx2; SNOMED CT set to null/none with gap note.
All three health context conditions populated with specific clinical guidance.
